item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concise. Original. A good reminder that brevity is the soul of wit.
Good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m going to second kirb in the second point. While there prolly exists amnestics outside. they are largely a foundation product. And if they existed outside the foundation and their likeminded peers, they would a: be called something else, and b: be quite rare. I think that the critics would have some meme resistance training instead. Or create counter memes. With the way it is now it feels unintentionally more meta than what I believe the article is intended to be. And it feels like the note at the end is a researcher’s note, not a art critic’s note.

I also distinctly remember a tale about a way to make non anomalous amnestics got leaked to the public and the disaster that ensued. But I can’t for the life of me find it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Knowing that only the Chinese and Japanese have translated it yet while both of them need to use more characters than ASCII provides makes me question myself. Nice format screw, thought someone would've come up with the idea sooner. +1 for being meta on the laziness of Series V.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
entertaining despite less words

+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The angry critique at the end made the article for me. I laughed a bunch at "2/5 would not recommend"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why use lot word when few make sense

+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Accept meme. Conveys best with few words.

Feel piece natural expression anart; feel response natural as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic title +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
meta

+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that’s a fair balance to have to strike, but I guess I’m coming from the place that, I felt like reading the ending was significantly easier than the beginning, which is not a good thing in this context.

Regarding the second point, my complaint is more that I don’t think they’d call them amnestics. It’s not a huge deal, but the terminology felt weird for an anartist critic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was hesitant to make the format screw any denser (especially at the end) for risk of frustrating readers. The format screw is, admittedly, the core of the article. I suppose I could have dragged it out longer, but again, reader frustration, but also the stilted writing style doesn't really lend itself to showing, well, anything.

While amnestics are a Foundation thing, I find it hard to believe that they are only the ones that access to them (at least, in a world where there are not only anartists, but anart critics), especially with them being dispensed with alarming regularity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The intended TL;DR of the article is the anartist is an idiot and doesn't understand minimalism, and the critic calls him out on it.

He's not making it for revenge, but because he likes minimalist art and is trying his own hand at it. The anartist is conflating artistic reductionism with minimalism, when this is obviously not the case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going back and forth on this one a lot. Not because of the format screw (I clicked this because I saw the title, so my inner complexity theory nerd kind of had a hay day), but because there are a few things that bothered me.

The main thing that bothered me was that, if you strip away the format screw, this falls pretty heavily into the "note-at-the-end" cliche. I think if you had minimized and abbreviated the notes at the end more, so the reader has so work a little more to understand the message, I probably wouldn't feel so much like you (the author) were telling me what the point of the article was directly. The last note in particular kind of struck me as "this is the author talking about minimalism" rather than the critic, just due to its directness. I know the directness is a side-effect of being concise, but I felt like you didn't minimize it enough to shake off the "telling instead of showing" feeling.

Two minor objections: by using the term "author note", I kind of thought you meant 4th wall breaking author, since usually anartists are referred to as "artists". Like, even if this is a written work, by using the term "author" I did a solid double take.

Other minor objection: I could've sworn that amnestics were a Foundation thing? Or at least the terminology was a Foundation thing, so having the critic use that term threw me off.

However, although I really do think the piece could be strengthened… I couldn't not upvote it. Like, it's one of those ideas that are like, simple and clever at the same time. Also some of the text optimizations (like swapping out header names for just the first letter since the whole header can be inferred, that information can be done away with). And also the title reference to information theory is too good for me.

So yeah, I'm throwing this a +1. I feel like it could be better, but like… it's still pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm glad i actually saw the rating before just reflexively downvoting and going back to New Pages when there wasn't any formatting. a good format screw with an interesting story attached, although I'm getting a little bored with the "anartist is tired of not being recognized so they make art to get revenge on their critics" bit. (this pulled it off well, though)

item.title: Me think, why waste time say lot word when few word do trick?
(no title)
item.content:encoded:
i love it. I especially love that this was an artist's attempt to force the world around it into "true minimalism" that ultimately failed, because the details were not completely "removed," just "hidden" and easy enough to extrapolate from the few keywords given. it really summarizes the experience of reading the article in a way that I thought was neat.

needless to say, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I understand correctly, this is minimalism as an SCP.

Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: bit wordy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I genuinely don't know how I feel about this. I can't tell if I hate this or love it, so no vote I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. Very concise. Easy to understand despite lack of info. Lost at the end. Think I get it though. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I like it.

item.title: Not a Coldpost, I swear
item.content:encoded:
A format screw I've been holding onto for at least a year.

Every reviewer I've talked to has said "This is weird and kind of neat, but I don't know if I'd upvote."

So let's find out.
